turns.content: I'm trying to port an old C++ project into Rust, and I noticed that when a panic happens in the new code, it just prints a generic message instead of showing the stack trace like I used to get from backtrace::Backtrace. It feels like my app is swallowing errors rather than exposing them.

That's a common friction point when moving from C++ to Rust, since modern Rust defaults to swallowing panics in release builds for performance. To capture the actual stack trace you're seeing in C++, you would use Backtrace::capture() on the panic hook or manually invoke it within your error handling logic.
Wait, I thought backtrace was just a standard library function that automatically prints when an error occurs? Isn't there a built-in way to get this without writing custom hooks?

You're correct that the std::panic! macro can be configured with RUST_BACKTRACE=1 to enable automatic stack trace printing, but it's not always sufficient for your specific error handling patterns. If you need to embed the backtrace into a custom Error type or want more control over when and how it is captured, you'll need to manually call Backtrace::capture() and store that result in your data structures.
But I'm confused about the cost of capture. I thought capturing a backtrace was just like calling backtrace() on a thread, which is cheap? Is it really expensive to embed this in every error?

The cost of capture is actually significant because Backtrace::capture() performs a full stack walk, which involves allocating memory and iterating through the call frame. While you can use backtrace() methods on Error trait implementations via nightly features for lightweight alternatives, embedding a full Backtrace struct in every error type will increase heap usage and slow down error propagation.
I see, so I should avoid using the expensive capture method everywhere? But then how do I debug production issues without seeing where they originated?

For production environments, you can keep RUST_BACKTRACE=1 enabled globally to catch panics automatically, and only manually invoke Backtrace::capture() in critical error paths like network failures or data corruption where detailed context is needed. You might also consider using the backtrace method on Error trait if available via nightly updates to reduce overhead.

That makes sense, so I should only use manual capture for critical errors and rely on environment variables for general panics? But what happens when someone tries to deserialize an error with a full backtrace in my serde_json::Value wrapper?

Deserializing errors containing Backtrace structs into JSON can be problematic because the backtrace data is not serializable by default. You would need to implement custom Serialize and Deserialize traits for your error type or strip the backtrace fields before serialization, otherwise serde_json::Value will fail to parse the struct.

I'm reviewing a new logging crate I wrote and I want to trace where every panic originates, but I have this hypothetical scenario in mind: if I call backtrace::capture() inside an error constructor on nightly, does it automatically embed the Backtrace into the Error struct so that when someone calls backtrace() on that error later, they get the full stack?

That's a great question about how std::backtrace::Backtrace works with custom errors. Think of it like this: calling capture() is similar to taking a photograph at the moment you create the error, but if you don't store that photo inside your struct, later people can't see it unless they have access to the camera. The cost of capture is memory and CPU because it builds a frame-by-frame list of stack locations, so doing it too early in a tight loop could hurt performance. You should embed the Backtrace into your custom error type only when you actually need that trace for debugging.

I see, but what if I'm using std::error::Error and want to call backtrace() on it later? If I don't manually embed the Backtrace in my custom type, will the Error trait's backtrace method automatically do this for me when I invoke it?

No, calling backtrace() on an Error that doesn't contain a captured Backtrace won't magically generate one. The RUST_BACKTRACE environment variable controls how the runtime dumps traces during panics or unwinding, but it does not replace your manual capture logic when you construct errors at runtime. If your custom error needs to store a stack trace for later inspection, you must embed the Backtrace in its fields; otherwise the backtrace() method will just return empty data because nothing was captured to begin with.
So if I'm building a custom error type that wraps another error, should I call capture() once at construction time and reuse that same Backtrace instance in every variant of my enum?

Exactly. If you're wrapping multiple errors in an enum variant, calling capture() once when the outer error is created ensures all variants share the same backtrace snapshot. You can then store that Backtrace alongside your inner errors and retrieve it later via backtrace() on the custom type. This avoids repeated stack traces and keeps memory usage predictable.

One last thing: if I set RUST_BACKTRACE=1 globally, does that make calling capture() unnecessary because the environment variable will automatically include my error's location in any future crash dumps?

RUST_BACKTRACE only affects panic unwinding and runtime trace dumps; it doesn't retroactively add backtrace data to your custom error types. If you want the exact stack at capture time stored in your error for later analysis, you must still call capture() manually. The environment variable helps with crash reports but won replace your explicit Backtrace embedding logic.
